threats.title: transferToUnoccupiedPlot Missing plotId Update Permanently Bricks Landlord Plots
threats.hypothesis_description: transferToUnoccupiedPlot updates plotOccupied for old and new plots but never updates toilerState[tokenId].plotId to the new plotId. When the renter subsequently calls unstakeMunchable, it reads the stale plotId from toilerState and clears the already-empty old plot, leaving the new plot permanently marked as occupied with no way to ever clear it. A malicious renter can chain transfers through ALL of a landlord's plots to brick them entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete missing state update bug. The code clearly writes plotOccupied[landlord][plotId] but never writes toilerState[tokenId].plotId = plotId. Any renter calling transferToUnoccupiedPlot followed by unstakeMunchable will trigger this permanently.

proof_of_signal.pattern: plotOccupied updated for old and new plotIds, but toilerState[tokenId].plotId is never assigned the new plotId value

preconditions: Renter has a staked Munchable NFT on a landlord's plot
At least one other unoccupied plot exists on the same landlord
Contract is not paused

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After transfer, toilerState.plotId remains the old plotId while plotOccupied reflects the new plotId, causing a permanent desync
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action can fix a bricked plot; plotOccupied has no manual override
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanently exploitable by any renter at any time

operational_questions: Can a single renter chain transfers through N plots, bricking N-1 of them before unstaking?
Is there any admin function in the base contract or proxy that can manually clear plotOccupied?
How many landlord plots could be bricked per transaction?
validation_requirements: Write test: stake on plot 0, transfer to plot 1, unstake. Verify plotOccupied[landlord][1] remains occupied=true permanently
Write test: chain transfer through plots 0→1→2→3→unstake. Verify plots 1, 2, 3 are all bricked
Verify no alternative code path can clear orphaned plotOccupied entries

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Stake Munchable NFT on landlord's plot 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call transferToUnoccupiedPlot(tokenId, 1) — plot 0 cleared, plot 1 occupied, toilerState.plotId still 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Repeat transferToUnoccupiedPlot for plots 2, 3, ... N-1, each bricking the previous plot
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call unstakeMunchable — clears stale plotId (plot 0, already empty), leaves plot N-1 permanently occupied
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — single attacker, single transaction sequence

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No admin recovery path visible in contract
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All of a landlord's plots can be permanently bricked, destroying their ability to host renters and earn tax revenue
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add plot cleanup function or manual storage manipulation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: stakeMunchable (reverts OccupiedPlotError on bricked plots), entire landlord staking capacity

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Monitor transferToUnoccupiedPlot events followed by unstake events for the same token
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: plotOccupied entries with occupied=true but no corresponding toilerState pointing to them
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Sequential transfer + unstake patterns from same address targeting same landlord

threats.title: Timestamp Underflow in Dirty Flag Path Permanently Locks NFTs in Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: In _farmPlots, when a plot becomes invalid (landlord reduced locked value), the code sets timestamp = plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated. If the renter farmed more recently than the landlord's last metadata update (lastToilDate > lastUpdated), the expression `timestamp - _toiler.lastToilDate` underflows in Solidity 0.8.25, causing a revert. Since unstakeMunchable calls forceFarmPlots, this revert prevents the renter from ever unstaking their NFT — permanently locking it in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The subtraction `(timestamp - _toiler.lastToilDate)` has no underflow protection. The dirty path sets timestamp to a historical value that can be earlier than lastToilDate. With Solidity 0.8.25 checked arithmetic, this reverts rather than wrapping.

proof_of_signal.pattern: timestamp = plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated (line ~240); then schnibblesTotal = (timestamp - _toiler.lastToilDate) * BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE (line ~256) — underflows if lastUpdated < lastToilDate

preconditions: Renter has staked NFT on a landlord's plot
Renter has farmed (setting lastToilDate) at time T2
Landlord's plotMetadata.lastUpdated was set at time T1 < T2
Landlord reduces locked value so _getNumPlots(landlord) < toiler.plotId

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Landlord's metadata lastUpdated can be arbitrarily old relative to renter's lastToilDate; no check prevents timestamp < lastToilDate
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No emergency unstake function exists; forceFarmPlots modifier is mandatory on unstake path
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — once the condition exists, the NFT can never be unstaked

operational_questions: How frequently do landlords reduce their locked value in practice?
Is there any code path to unstake without going through _farmPlots?
Can a landlord intentionally trigger this to grief a renter?
validation_requirements: Test: landlord's lastUpdated at T=100, renter farms at T=200, landlord reduces plots at T=300. Verify farmPlots reverts
Verify unstakeMunchable also reverts (due to forceFarmPlots modifier)
Verify no alternative unstake or rescue path exists

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Landlord stakes value creating plots, metadata lastUpdated = T1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Renter stakes NFT on high plot, farms at T2 > T1 (lastToilDate = T2)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Landlord unlocks value, reducing plot count below renter's plotId
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Renter attempts to farm or unstake — dirty path triggers, timestamp = T1, subtraction T1 - T2 underflows, transaction reverts. NFT permanently locked.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Can occur naturally (landlord unlocks unaware of impact) or be triggered maliciously by landlord

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None required — this is a code bug in normal operation flow
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: NFTs permanently locked in contract; renter loses their asset with no recovery
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add emergency unstake or fix the underflow
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: unstakeMunchable, farmPlots, stakeMunchable, transferToUnoccupiedPlot — all use forceFarmPlots

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — can occur naturally or be triggered by landlord
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires landlord to reduce locked value after renter's last farm

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Staked tokens where lastToilDate > plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated AND plot is invalid
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Landlords withdrawing locked value while having active renters on high-index plots

threats.title: Schnibbles Reward Formula Systematically Deflated by 100x Due to Incorrect Math
threats.hypothesis_description: The schnibbles calculation uses `(schnibblesTotal + schnibblesTotal * finalBonus) / 100` which computes `schnibblesTotal * (1 + finalBonus) / 100`. When finalBonus = 0 (no bonus), the result is `schnibblesTotal / 100` — 1% of the expected base rate. The correct formula should be `schnibblesTotal + (schnibblesTotal * finalBonus / 100)` = `schnibblesTotal * (100 + finalBonus) / 100`. This means ALL users receive approximately 100x fewer rewards than intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula is demonstrably wrong: at zero bonus, users should receive 100% of base rewards but instead receive 1%. This is a straightforward arithmetic error that affects every single farming operation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: schnibblesTotal = uint256((int256(schnibblesTotal) + (int256(schnibblesTotal) * finalBonus)) / 100) — divides the ENTIRE expression by 100 instead of only the bonus portion

preconditions: Any user with staked Munchable NFTs
Any configuration of REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Every farmPlots call produces ~1% of expected schnibbles, universally affecting all renters and landlords
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Cannot be fixed via configuration; requires code fix
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Active since deployment, affects every farming operation

operational_questions: Has this been compensated for by setting BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE 100x higher than intended?
Are REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES values scaled to account for this formula?
What is the actual schnibbles emission rate vs the designed rate?
validation_requirements: Compute expected schnibbles for a 1-hour stake with zero bonus and compare to actual
Verify the formula: with finalBonus=0, schnibblesTotal should equal base rate * time, not base_rate * time / 100
Check if BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE is intentionally inflated by 100x to compensate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack per se — this is a universal miscalculation affecting all participants
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All renters receive 1/100th of intended schnibbles when bonus is 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: With finalBonus=99, users receive schnibblesTotal*100/100 = schnibblesTotal (the intended no-bonus rate only at +99% bonus)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Economic model is fundamentally broken — bonus system required to achieve even baseline rewards

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All schnibbles rewards are ~100x lower than designed, breaking the game economy
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code upgrade; past rewards cannot be retroactively corrected

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare actual schnibbles minted vs expected based on BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE * time
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Universally low reward rates across all stakers

threats.title: Off-by-One in Dirty Flag Check Allows Farming on Non-Existent Boundary Plot
threats.hypothesis_description: The dirty flag check in _farmPlots uses `_getNumPlots(landlord) < _toiler.plotId` instead of `<=`. Since plot IDs are 0-indexed, when _getNumPlots returns N, valid plots are 0 through N-1. A toiler on plotId N should be flagged dirty, but the check N < N evaluates false, allowing the renter to continue farming indefinitely on a plot that no longer exists.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear off-by-one error. With 5 plots (indices 0-4), a renter on plotId 5 should be invalid, but 5 < 5 is false so they continue farming normally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_getNumPlots(landlord) < _toiler.plotId) — should be <= since plotId is 0-indexed and _getNumPlots returns count

preconditions: Renter staked on plotId N where N was valid at stake time
Landlord reduces locked value so _getNumPlots returns exactly N
Renter calls farmPlots

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Renter earns rewards on a plot that no longer exists, backed by no locked value
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Persists as long as numPlots equals exactly the renter's plotId

operational_questions: How often do landlords reduce locked value by exactly one plot's worth?
Can a renter intentionally stake on the highest plot to exploit this?
What is the economic impact of unbacked reward generation?
validation_requirements: Test: landlord has 5 plots, renter on plot 4, landlord reduces to 4 plots. Verify renter still farms without dirty flag
Verify stakeMunchable correctly uses < for bounds (plotId >= totalPlotsAvail), creating asymmetry with dirty check

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Stake on the highest valid plotId for a landlord
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for landlord to reduce locked value by one plot's worth
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Continue farming at full rate on the now-invalid boundary plot
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — occurs naturally; can be actively positioned

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unbacked reward inflation at boundary; unfair advantage for boundary renters

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Depends on landlord reducing locked value

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Farming events on plot IDs at the boundary of landlord capacity

threats.title: Staking Limit Off-by-One Allows 11 NFTs Instead of Intended 10
threats.hypothesis_description: The staking limit check uses `munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10` which allows staking when length is exactly 10, resulting in 11 total staked NFTs after the push. The intended maximum appears to be 10 based on the constant, requiring `>= 10` instead of `> 10`.
threats.investigation_rationale: The check `> 10` is evaluated BEFORE the push operation, so when length is 10, the condition is false, and the 11th NFT is successfully staked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (munchablesStaked[mainAccount].length > 10) revert TooManyStakedMunchiesError() — allows length 10 to pass, resulting in 11 after push

preconditions: User has 10 already-staked Munchable NFTs
User owns an 11th NFT to stake
Available plot on any landlord

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: User can exceed the intended staking capacity by one NFT

operational_questions: What is the intended maximum — 10 or 11?
Does the uint8 loop iterator in _farmPlots handle 11 iterations correctly? (Yes — 11 < 255)
Does the extra slot provide meaningful economic advantage?
validation_requirements: Test: stake 10 NFTs, verify 11th can be staked
Verify farmPlots handles 11 staked tokens without issues

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Stake 11th NFT — succeeds due to > instead of >= check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Earn 10% more rewards than intended maximum allows

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Minor — one extra staked NFT per user

threats.title: Negative Bonus Int256-to-Uint256 Cast Produces Near-Infinite Schnibbles
threats.hypothesis_description: The schnibbles calculation casts a potentially negative int256 result to uint256: `uint256((int256(schnibblesTotal) + (int256(schnibblesTotal) * finalBonus)) / 100)`. If the combined REALM_BONUSES + RARITY_BONUSES (cast from uint8 to int8) produce a finalBonus <= -2, the numerator becomes negative. Casting a negative int256 to uint256 produces a value near 2^256, granting the attacker near-infinite schnibbles in a single farm call.
threats.investigation_rationale: RARITY_BONUSES is a uint8 array. When cast via `int8(RARITY_BONUSES[...])`, values 128-255 become -128 to -1. Combined with negative REALM_BONUSES entries, finalBonus can easily be <= -2. There is no check that the int256 result is non-negative before the uint256 cast.
deployment_assumptions: REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES arrays contain values that can produce finalBonus <= -2 for at least one realm/rarity combination
threats.config_evidence: LandManager.sol lines 253-257; REALM_BONUSES loaded from configStorage.getSmallIntArray(StorageKey.RealmBonuses), RARITY_BONUSES from configStorage.getSmallUintArray(StorageKey.RarityBonuses). No deployment script shows actual configured values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: finalBonus computed from int16 REALM_BONUSES and int8-cast RARITY_BONUSES; result cast to uint256 without negativity check: uint256((int256(schnibblesTotal) + (int256(schnibblesTotal) * finalBonus)) / 100)

preconditions: REALM_BONUSES and/or RARITY_BONUSES configuration produces finalBonus <= -2 for at least one combination
Attacker owns an NFT with the matching realm/rarity
Attacker stakes on a landlord with the matching snuggeryRealm

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No explicit check: `if (result < 0) result = 0;` before uint256 cast
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on ConfigStorage bonus array values
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Once config allows negative combo, any matching NFT holder can exploit on every farm

operational_questions: What are the actual deployed REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES arrays?
Are there any realm/rarity combinations that produce finalBonus <= -2?
Does the ConfigStorage enforce any constraints on bonus array values?
validation_requirements: Retrieve deployed REALM_BONUSES and RARITY_BONUSES arrays
Enumerate all (realm × snuggeryRealm × rarity) combinations and compute finalBonus
Test: set finalBonus = -2, farm, verify schnibbles output is astronomical

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Obtain or mint NFT with that realm/rarity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Stake on landlord whose snuggeryRealm creates the negative bonus
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Farm — receive near 2^256 schnibbles, breaking game economy
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None if attacker already holds matching NFT; may need to acquire specific NFT

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete destruction of schnibbles economy — infinite inflation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Extremely difficult — past schnibbles cannot be clawed back without manual state intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All reward distribution, game economy integrity

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — single user, single farm call
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Intermediate — must identify correct realm/rarity combo
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after config allows negative bonus

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Abnormally large schnibbles amounts in FarmedSchnibbles events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Single farm call generating billions of schnibbles
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific realm/rarity NFTs being targeted for staking

threats.title: PRICE_PER_PLOT Zero from Config Mismatch Causes Complete Contract DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: _getNumPlots divides by PRICE_PER_PLOT. If PRICE_PER_PLOT is 0 (loaded from incorrect StorageKey.NFTOverlord which may store a contract address or zero), every function calling _getNumPlots reverts with division by zero. Since _farmPlots calls _getNumPlots, and forceFarmPlots is a modifier on stakeMunchable, unstakeMunchable, and transferToUnoccupiedPlot, ALL staking operations become impossible. Worse, existing NFTs cannot be unstaked — they are permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: PRICE_PER_PLOT is loaded from StorageKey.NFTOverlord using getUint, but NFTOverlord is semantically an address key. If ConfigStorage returns 0 for an unrecognized or incorrectly typed key, division by zero occurs.
deployment_assumptions: ConfigStorage.getUint(StorageKey.NFTOverlord) returns 0 or the key-value mapping stores addresses where uints are expected
threats.config_evidence: LandManager.sol lines 72-73: PRICE_PER_PLOT = IConfigStorage(configStorage).getUint(StorageKey.NFTOverlord); line 284: return lockManager.getLockedWeightedValue(_account) / PRICE_PER_PLOT;

proof_of_signal.pattern: PRICE_PER_PLOT loaded from address-type StorageKey.NFTOverlord; used as divisor in _getNumPlots with no zero check

preconditions: ConfigStorage.getUint(StorageKey.NFTOverlord) returns 0
Any function calling _getNumPlots is invoked

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: PRICE_PER_PLOT loaded from semantically wrong key, no validation for > 0
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: ConfigStorage admin can fix by setting the correct value, but existing locked NFTs are stuck until fixed
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately on deployment if config is wrong; or after any configUpdated call

operational_questions: What does ConfigStorage.getUint(StorageKey.NFTOverlord) actually return on the deployed contract?
Is there dual-use of StorageKeys for both addresses and uints in ConfigStorage?
How quickly can config be updated to fix a zero PRICE_PER_PLOT?
validation_requirements: Query deployed ConfigStorage for getUint(StorageKey.NFTOverlord)
Test _getNumPlots with PRICE_PER_PLOT = 0 — verify division by zero revert
Test unstakeMunchable with PRICE_PER_PLOT = 0 — verify NFT cannot be retrieved

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: If config returns 0: all staking operations revert immediately
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users with already-staked NFTs cannot unstake (forceFarmPlots → _farmPlots → _getNumPlots → revert)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: NFTs permanently locked until config is fixed and contract upgraded or config corrected
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: N/A — this is a configuration-triggered DoS, not an active attack

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete protocol freeze — no staking, unstaking, farming, or transfers possible
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium — requires correct config value to be set; NFTs recoverable after fix

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Immediately on deployment or after configUpdated

threats.title: Configuration Key Mismatch Loads Wrong Values for All Numeric Parameters
threats.hypothesis_description: _reconfigure loads MIN_TAX_RATE from StorageKey.LockManager, MAX_TAX_RATE from StorageKey.AccountManager, DEFAULT_TAX_RATE from StorageKey.ClaimManager, BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE from StorageKey.MigrationManager, and PRICE_PER_PLOT from StorageKey.NFTOverlord. These keys are semantically designed for contract addresses, not numeric configuration values. If ConfigStorage's getUint returns the address as uint256, MIN_TAX_RATE could be a huge address-derived number making all tax updates revert, or values could be zero breaking multiple subsystems.
threats.investigation_rationale: The semantic mismatch between key names and their usage is evident in the code. Five critical numeric parameters are loaded from keys named after contract managers.
deployment_assumptions: ConfigStorage uses the same StorageKey enum for both address and uint lookups, and getUint returns the address value cast to uint256
threats.config_evidence: LandManager.sol lines 65-73: MIN_TAX_RATE = getUint(StorageKey.LockManager), MAX_TAX_RATE = getUint(StorageKey.AccountManager), DEFAULT_TAX_RATE = getUint(StorageKey.ClaimManager), BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE = getUint(StorageKey.MigrationManager), PRICE_PER_PLOT = getUint(StorageKey.NFTOverlord)

proof_of_signal.pattern: getUint called with StorageKey.LockManager, StorageKey.AccountManager etc. — names indicating contract address keys, used for uint config values

preconditions: ConfigStorage stores addresses and uints in separate namespaces OR the getUint/getAddress functions share the same key-value store
The deployed ConfigStorage returns unexpected values for these keys when queried via getUint

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Five critical parameters loaded from keys semantically belonging to contract addresses
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Effective from deployment; every configUpdated call reinforces wrong values

operational_questions: Does ConfigStorage separate address storage from uint storage using the same enum?
What are the actual values returned by getUint for these address-type keys?
Is this a known pattern in the Munchables codebase where keys are intentionally reused?
validation_requirements: Read ConfigStorage source to understand getUint vs getAddress key separation
Query deployed contract: MIN_TAX_RATE, MAX_TAX_RATE, DEFAULT_TAX_RATE, BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE, PRICE_PER_PLOT
Verify whether returned values are sensible for their intended purpose

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an active attack — misconfiguration at protocol level
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If MIN_TAX_RATE > MAX_TAX_RATE (e.g., address-derived), all updateTaxRate calls revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If DEFAULT_TAX_RATE is outside [MIN, MAX], new landlords get invalid tax rates
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If BASE_SCHNIBBLE_RATE is an address (~10^20), rewards are astronomically inflated

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Multiple subsystems broken: tax updates, reward calculations, plot computations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium — requires either ConfigStorage fix or contract upgrade
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All tax, reward, and plot calculations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Anomalous tax rates, reward amounts, or plot counts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: State variables containing address-sized numbers where small config values expected

threats.title: Dirty Flag Uses Latest Metadata Timestamp Instead of Plot Invalidation Time, Granting Excess Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: When a plot becomes invalid (landlord reduces locked value), the dirty mechanism uses plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated as the reward cutoff timestamp. If the landlord's metadata was updated AFTER the plot became invalid (e.g., new lock/unlock event), lastUpdated reflects the latest update rather than when the plot first became invalid. A renter who hasn't farmed in a while receives rewards for the entire period up to lastUpdated, not just the period when the plot was valid.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol documentation acknowledges this as a 'known accepted risk' but the impact can be significant: a renter could receive weeks of extra rewards if the landlord's metadata is updated frequently after the plot invalidation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: timestamp = plotMetadata[landlord].lastUpdated — uses the LATEST metadata update time, not the time when the plot first became invalid

preconditions: Renter staked on a plot that becomes invalid (landlord reduces locked value)
Landlord's metadata is updated at least once after plot invalidation
Renter hasn't farmed between plot invalidation and metadata update
The resulting timestamp > lastToilDate (otherwise underflow occurs per OPS-H002)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: lastUpdated can advance arbitrarily after plot invalidation, extending the reward window
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: updatePlotMetadata called by AccountManager on various events
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Proportional to gap between plot invalidation and latest metadata update

operational_questions: How frequently is updatePlotMetadata called for active landlords?
What events trigger AccountManager to call updatePlotMetadata?
What is the maximum realistic reward overpayment from this bug?
validation_requirements: Trace all code paths that call updatePlotMetadata
Simulate: renter stakes day 1, plot invalidated day 5, metadata updated day 20, renter farms day 25
Verify renter receives 20 days of rewards instead of 5

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Stake on a landlord's highest plotId
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for landlord to reduce locked value (invalidating the plot)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for landlord's metadata to be updated by AccountManager for any reason
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Farm — receive rewards from stake time to lastUpdated rather than to invalidation time
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low — requires patience and monitoring landlord activity

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unbacked reward inflation; renters earn more than their plot duration warrants
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Would require tracking and clawing back excess schnibbles

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires gap between invalidation and metadata update

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Dirty-flagged tokens earning rewards beyond their plot validity period
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large time gaps between plot invalidation and farming
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Renters deliberately delaying farming after landlord reduces plots

threats.title: Tax Rate Above 1e18 Causes Underflow in Renter Share, Blocking All Farming and Locking NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: If a landlord's latestTaxRate exceeds 1e18 (100%), the calculation `schnibblesLandlord = (schnibblesTotal * latestTaxRate) / 1e18` produces schnibblesLandlord > schnibblesTotal. The subsequent `schnibblesTotal - schnibblesLandlord` then underflows in Solidity 0.8.25, reverting _farmPlots. Since forceFarmPlots is a modifier on unstakeMunchable, affected renters' NFTs become permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: If MIN_TAX_RATE and MAX_TAX_RATE are loaded from wrong config keys (OPS-H008) and result in values > 1e18, a landlord could set a rate exceeding 100%. The updateTaxRate bounds check only enforces [MIN_TAX_RATE, MAX_TAX_RATE] which may be misconfigured.
deployment_assumptions: MAX_TAX_RATE loaded from StorageKey.AccountManager is > 1e18 (e.g., address-derived value)
threats.config_evidence: LandManager.sol line 67: MAX_TAX_RATE = IConfigStorage(configStorage).getUint(StorageKey.AccountManager); line 259-260: schnibblesLandlord = (schnibblesTotal * _toiler.latestTaxRate) / 1e18; renter gets schnibblesTotal - schnibblesLandlord

proof_of_signal.pattern: schnibblesLandlord = (schnibblesTotal * _toiler.latestTaxRate) / 1e18; renterMetadata.unfedSchnibbles += (schnibblesTotal - schnibblesLandlord) — underflows if latestTaxRate > 1e18

preconditions: MAX_TAX_RATE > 1e18 due to config key mismatch
Landlord calls updateTaxRate with value > 1e18
Renter's toilerState.latestTaxRate gets updated to > 1e18 on next farm

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Tax rate bounds derived from potentially address-typed config values
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on ConfigStorage returning correct bounds values
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After landlord sets rate > 1e18, all their renters are affected on next farm

operational_questions: What is the actual deployed MAX_TAX_RATE value?
Has any landlord set a tax rate > 1e18?
Can the config be corrected without upgrading the contract?
validation_requirements: Query deployed MAX_TAX_RATE value
Test: set latestTaxRate to 2e18, attempt farmPlots — verify revert
Verify unstakeMunchable also reverts in this scenario

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Landlord calls updateTaxRate with value > 1e18 (only possible if MAX_TAX_RATE allows it)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Renter farms — latestTaxRate updated to > 1e18 for next period
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Renter tries to farm again or unstake — subtraction underflows, reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: NFT permanently locked until contract upgrade or config fix + manual state repair
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires landlord action; could be malicious or accidental if config allows

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All renters on the landlord's plots have NFTs permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High — requires contract upgrade and state migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Farming, unstaking for affected landlord's renters

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires landlord to set rate

threats.title: Landlord Tax Rate Bait-and-Switch Without Automatic Farming of Existing Stakers
threats.hypothesis_description: updateTaxRate does not trigger _farmPlots for existing stakers before changing the rate. While the latestTaxRate snapshot in toilerState protects the current accrued period, the rate change is immediately stored and will be applied to the NEXT farming period for all stakers. A landlord can attract renters with a low tax rate, then switch to MAX_TAX_RATE. The new rate takes effect from the renter's next farm call, giving the renter no way to retroactively protect their future accrual.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code updates plotMetadata[landlord].currentTaxRate directly without farming existing stakers. toilerState[tokenId].latestTaxRate is only updated when the renter farms, creating a window for rate manipulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: plotMetadata[landlord].currentTaxRate = newTaxRate — direct assignment with no call to _farmPlots for existing stakers on this landlord

preconditions: Landlord has stakers on their plots
Landlord changes tax rate
Renter farms after the rate change

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Rate change is stored globally while per-token rate is updated lazily on farm, creating a manipulation window
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Effective from rate change until renter reacts by unstaking

operational_questions: How frequently do landlords change tax rates in practice?
What is the maximum economic impact of a bait-and-switch?
Would forcing farming on rate change be gas-prohibitive?
validation_requirements: Test: landlord sets 1% rate, renter stakes and farms, landlord raises to MAX, renter farms again — verify second period uses MAX
Calculate maximum schnibbles lost to renter from a bait-and-switch

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Landlord sets attractive low tax rate to attract renters
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Renters stake and begin farming at low rate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Renters' next farm applies MAX rate for the entire period since last farm
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single landlord acting in bad faith

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unfair tax extraction from renters who relied on advertised rate
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low — renters can unstake but lose expected yield

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Most effective when renters haven't farmed recently

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rapid tax rate increases on landlords with active stakers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Landlords cycling between MIN and MAX rates
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Renters unstaking shortly after rate changes

threats.title: No Emergency Unstake Path — Paused Contract Permanently Traps All Custodied NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: Both stakeMunchable and unstakeMunchable are gated by the notPaused modifier. There is no emergency withdrawal function, rescue mechanism, or pause-exempt unstake path. If the contract is paused (legitimately or maliciously) and cannot be unpaused (admin key loss, governance deadlock, or intentional griefing by pause authority), all NFTs held in custody are permanently locked with no recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract holds custodied ERC721 NFTs via transferFrom. The only exit path (unstakeMunchable) requires the contract to be unpaused. No emergency rescue function exists in the visible code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unstakeMunchable has notPaused modifier; no alternative unstake or emergency rescue function exists in contract

preconditions: Contract is paused
Pause cannot be lifted (admin unavailability, key compromise, governance deadlock)
Users have staked NFTs in the contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: NFT custody locked behind same pause gate as all other operations — no separate emergency path
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Pause authority (inherited from BaseBlastManagerUpgradeable) controls all NFT recovery
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Duration of pause; permanent if unpause is impossible

operational_questions: Who controls the pause/unpause authority in BaseBlastManagerUpgradeable?
Is there a timelock or multisig on pause authority?
Does the proxy allow upgrade during pause to add emergency withdrawal?
validation_requirements: Review BaseBlastManagerUpgradeable for pause/unpause access control
Verify no emergency withdrawal in base contracts
Test: pause contract, attempt unstakeMunchable — verify revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Pause authority pauses the contract (legitimately or through compromise)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All unstakeMunchable calls revert with pause check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: NFT owners cannot recover their assets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If pause authority is lost, NFTs are permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires pause authority compromise or admin error

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Pause authority is the single point of failure for all NFT recovery
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All custodied NFTs inaccessible during pause
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High if unpause authority is lost; otherwise recoverable on unpause
